svg.text: BRITISH SIGN LANGUAGE
NOTATION — TWO-HANDED VS ONE-HANDED
BSL: BRITISH SIGN LANGUAGE
BSL: natural sign language of UK Deaf community; ~125,000 users
NOT related to ASL (despite both being English-country sign languages)
BSL and ASL are mutually unintelligible; different grammars and signs
BSL TWO-HANDED MANUAL ALPHABET
BSL fingerspelling: TWO-HANDED (ASL: one-handed)
Each letter: specific configuration of both hands together
Example: A = right fist on left palm • B = both hands flat, fingers together
Two-handed alphabet: more visually distinctive but requires both hands free
BSL GLOSSING AND NOTATION
Same glossing conventions as ASL: CAPS for signs, IX for index/pointing
BSL-specific: FS: = fingerspelling follows • MM = mouth morpheme
BSL corpus: University of Bristol BSL Corpus (survey of 249 signers)
BSL RECOGNITION
2003: UK Parliament recognizes BSL as official language of Great Britain
2022: BSL Act: BSL becomes statutory recognized language (Scotland 2015)
DGS · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
DEUTSCHE GEBAERDENSPRACHE (DGS)
DGS: sign language of Germany; ~200,000 users (Deaf + hearing)
2002: DGS recognized as official language in Germany
Also used in: Austria, Luxembourg (alongside local varieties)
DGS MANUAL ALPHABET
DGS: one-handed manual alphabet (like ASL, unlike BSL)
German manual alphabet: based on Franciscan traditions; some French SL influence
Includes umlauts: separate handshapes for ä, ö, ü
NOTATION COMPARISON
BSL: two-handed fingerspelling (unique among major sign languages)
DGS: one-handed (shared with ASL, LSF, most European sign languages)
Both: SignWriting and HamNoSys used in research
Both: glossing used in interpreting and linguistics (different gloss languages)
PLATE 63a ALT · BSL / DGS · NOTATION
